skill.name: dispatching-parallel-agents
skill.description: Guide AI assistants to split independent debugging or development tasks across multiple focused agents.
skill.summary: Coordinate parallel agents for independent software investigations.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged fenced Markdown examples, diagram syntax, and ordinary workflow text as risky patterns. Manual review found no executable scripts, shell commands, network calls, cryptography, reconnaissance behavior, or prompt injection attempts in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced Blocks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The flagged backtick locations are Markdown fences for a DOT diagram, TypeScript-style Task examples, a prompt example, and a dispatch example. They are documentation examples, not shell execution or runtime code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are Markdown code fence delimiters or documentation examples. No Ruby, shell, or executable command path is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Line 3 is the skill description metadata about independent tasks and sequential dependencies. No cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic implementation appears on that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line contains plain YAML description text. There is no hash function, cipher, key handling, or security-sensitive cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: Line 60 describes a clear agent goal, and line 169 tells users to check whether agents edited the same code. These are coordination instructions, not system or network reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both lines are ordinary workflow guidance for scoped debugging tasks. No commands, host discovery, port scanning, or environment inspection are present.

content.value_statement: Complex debugging sessions slow down when independent failures are handled one at a time. This skill helps Claude, Codex, or Claude Code split unrelated work into focused parallel agent tasks.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when failures are independent enough for parallel investigation.
Groups test failures by file, subsystem, or problem domain.
Provides a structure for focused agent prompts with scope and constraints.
Explains when sequential investigation is safer than parallel dispatch.
Guides review and integration after multiple agents return results.
Shows concrete debugging examples for race conditions and batch behavior.
limitations: Does not run agents by itself or provide an automation script.
Requires the user to decide whether tasks truly have independent state.
Does not prevent conflicts if agents edit overlapping files.
Works best for software debugging and testing workflows.

use_cases.title: Split Independent Test Failures
use_cases.description: Turn several unrelated failing test files into scoped agent assignments with clear expected outputs.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Refactor Follow-up
use_cases.description: Assign separate agents to inspect independent subsystems after a large refactor introduces isolated issues.

use_cases.title: Teach Multi-Agent Workflow Design
use_cases.description: Show teams how to write narrow prompts, avoid shared-state conflicts, and integrate parallel findings.

prompt_templates.title: Find Independent Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these failures and group them by independent problem domain. Tell me which groups can be assigned to separate agents.

prompt_templates.title: Create Agent Briefs
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare focused tasks for parallel debugging.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create one agent prompt per independent failure group. Include scope, goal, constraints, files to inspect, and expected summary.

prompt_templates.title: Review Parallel Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these agent summaries. Identify conflicts, overlapping edits, missing verification, and the order for integrating changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Full Dispatch Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a complex debugging session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a parallel agent dispatch plan for this failing suite. Separate independent domains, define prompts, set integration checks, and list final verification steps.

output_examples.input: Three unrelated test files fail after a refactor.
output: Group one covers abort behavior and should get its own agent.
Group two covers batch completion and can run in parallel.
Group three covers approval race conditions and needs a narrow prompt.
Run the full suite after integrating all returned fixes.

output_examples.input: Two failures touch the same shared state manager.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend sequential investigation first because both failures may share one root cause.

output_examples.input: An agent returns a fix summary without verification.
output_examples.output: The skill directs the user to review the summary, check for conflicts, and run the full suite before accepting the changes.

best_practices: Dispatch agents only when each task has a separate scope and no shared state.
Give each agent exact files, failure names, constraints, and expected output.
Review all summaries and run complete verification before merging results.
anti_patterns: Do not use parallel agents when one fix may resolve several failures.
Do not give broad prompts such as fixing all tests without boundaries.
Do not integrate agent changes without checking for overlapping edits.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan parallel AI agent work for independent debugging and development tasks.

faq.question: Does it execute commands or start agents automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill that guides prompt design and coordination.

faq.question: When should I avoid using it?
faq.answer: Avoid it when failures are related, share state, or require one complete system investigation.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is written for Claude Code-style task dispatch and also applies to Claude and Codex.

faq.question: How many agents should I dispatch?
faq.answer: Use one agent per independent problem domain, not one agent per symptom.

faq.question: What should I do after agents return?
faq.answer: Read each summary, check for conflicts, run the full test suite, and spot check the fixes.
